title: ASU Counseling Services
summary: Counseling is a normal support option for stress, anxiety, adjustment, grief, overwhelm, and the general feeling that everything is heavier than it should be.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Use the counseling site to start, then attend an in-person intake or first appointment at your campus location.

steps.title: Start on the counseling site
steps.description: Begin with the ASU counseling page so you can tell whether you need urgent help or a routine appointment.
bullets: Open the counseling services page.
Check whether your need is urgent or routine.
Follow the appointment or contact instructions for your campus.

visual.title: Urgent support versus routine support
visual.caption: The first decision is usually just whether this is immediate crisis support or a standard appointment.

steps.title: Arrive with the basics, not a perfect explanation
steps.description: You do not need a polished story to go in. A few real sentences about what is going on is enough.
bullets: Show up a few minutes early if you have a scheduled appointment.
Bring your ASU ID and any intake instructions you received.
Start with what feels hardest right now: stress, sleep, panic, homesickness, or not feeling okay.
steps.reassurance: Students often worry they are “not struggling enough” to use counseling. That is not how this works.

visual.title: Quiet waiting area and first conversation
visual.caption: The first visit usually feels slower and more conversational than people expect.

steps.title: Use the first conversation to name the pattern
steps.description: The first session is usually about understanding what is happening and what kind of support would help next.
bullets: Say when the stress or symptoms are showing up most.
Mention school impacts like missed class, sleep problems, or feeling detached.
Ask what the next step usually looks like after this first visit.

visual.title: Turning a vague bad feeling into a clearer support plan
visual.caption: You do not have to solve everything in one visit. You are starting the process.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: If a virtual visit feels easier, start through the counseling site and follow the online intake or meeting instructions.

steps.title: Choose the virtual path
steps.description: Use the counseling site or your appointment instructions to confirm the online meeting process.
bullets: Check whether your appointment or intake step is virtual.
Save the meeting link or online instructions.
Make sure you know whether you need to complete any forms first.

visual.title: Private space and the right link
visual.caption: The biggest practical need is a quiet place where you can talk honestly.

steps.title: Set up a private, low-pressure space
steps.description: Before the session, choose the quietest place you can manage and keep water, headphones, or notes nearby.
bullets: Join from a private room, parked car, or another space where you can talk.
Use headphones if that makes you feel safer.
Write down the first thing you want to say in case your mind blanks.
steps.reassurance: It is common to feel nervous right before joining. You are allowed to start awkwardly and take a second to settle in.

visual.title: Small setup changes can make the session easier
visual.caption: You do not need a perfect room. You just need enough privacy to be real.

steps.title: Name what feels most urgent right now
steps.description: The session will usually go better if you start with what is hitting you hardest this week.
bullets: Talk about sleep, stress spikes, panic, loneliness, motivation loss, or feeling numb if those are true.
Mention if school is already being affected.
Ask what support options make the most sense after this first conversation.

visual.title: Start with the part that feels hardest to carry alone
visual.caption: You do not need to summarize your entire life to make the meeting useful.

modes.label: Drop-in
modes.summary: Use same-day or urgent support when you need to talk to someone sooner instead of waiting for a routine appointment.

steps.title: Decide whether you need support sooner
steps.description: If waiting feels unsafe or unrealistic, use the faster contact option instead of trying to push through it.
bullets: Check the counseling site for urgent help and immediate contact info.
Use same-day or urgent support if your distress is climbing fast.
If you are in crisis, follow the crisis instructions instead of waiting for a normal appointment.

visual.title: You do not have to wait until things get worse
visual.caption: Urgent support exists because some days should not be handled alone.

steps.title: Start with the simplest true sentence
steps.description: When you reach someone, begin with the clearest true thing you can say right now.
bullets: Try: “I am overwhelmed and it is starting to affect school.”
Or: “I am not in immediate danger, but I do not think I should handle this by myself.”
Let the staff guide the next questions.
steps.reassurance: You do not need to perform distress correctly. Honest and simple is enough.

visual.title: One honest sentence opens the door
visual.caption: You are allowed to ask for help before the situation becomes unmanageable.

steps.title: Follow the next recommended step the same day
steps.description: If they recommend follow-up care, a same-day action plan, or another office, try to set that up before the day ends.
bullets: Write down the next contact, appointment, or coping instruction.
Save any phone numbers or links they give you.
If they recommend a follow-up appointment, schedule it while the conversation is still fresh.

visual.title: The next step matters as much as the first contact
visual.caption: When you are overwhelmed, a written plan is part of the support.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I think I need some support and I am not totally sure how this process works yet.
questionSet.defaultProTip: If your mind goes blank, start with how this is affecting sleep, class attendance, concentration, or your ability to function day to day.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: Many students use counseling for the first time in college. You do not need to wait for a crisis to count.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: My stress about school is getting bad enough that it is affecting my focus and sleep.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: Money stress is hitting me hard and I am starting to feel anxious all the time.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: I am overwhelmed most days and I do not feel like I am bouncing back on my own.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: I am stretched too thin and I am starting to feel like everything is slipping.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I know I am not doing well, but I am having trouble explaining exactly why.

first-year: What usually happens in a first counseling appointment here?
How do students usually know whether they should keep coming back?
What can I do this week if stress is already affecting classes or sleep?
Is there a normal next step after today?
What is one sign that I should reach out again sooner rather than later?
second-year: How can I tell whether what I am feeling is something I should keep getting support for?
What should I do when stress starts affecting school habits again?
What support options make the most sense for what I described?
How do I follow up if I need more help after today?
What is one thing students usually do too late that I could do earlier?
third-year: What kind of support would be most practical with my current academic load?
How do I keep stress from taking over the rest of the semester?
What should I do if I notice this affecting motivation, attendance, or concentration more?
How do I know whether follow-up support is the right move for me?
What campus support pairs well with counseling if school is part of the problem?
fourth-year-plus: What support strategy makes sense if stress is hitting during a high-stakes semester?
How do I handle this without letting it quietly affect graduation progress?
What should I watch for if I am telling myself I can just push through it?
What is the best next step if I need support again after this visit?
How can I tell the difference between normal stress and something I should not handle alone?
